infobox.value: China | [The Great Wall of China (Mutianyﺁ section)](/wiki/File:The_Great_Wall_of_China_at_Jinshanling.jpg "The Great Wall of China (Mutianyﺁ section)") | 1368 AD
[Ma'an](/wiki/Ma%27an_Governorate "Ma'an Governorate"), Jordan | [Ad Deir ("The Monastery")](/wiki/File:The_Monastery,_Petra,_Jordan8.jpg "Ad Deir (\"The Monastery\")") | 40 AD

[Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), Italy | [The Colosseum at dusk: exterior view of the best-preserved section](/wiki/File:Rome_Colosseum_exterior_panorama.jpg "The Colosseum at dusk: exterior view of the best-preserved section") | 80 AD

[Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_(state) "Yucatán (state)"), Mexico | [El Castillo being climbed by tourists](/wiki/File:Chichen-Itza-Castillo-Seen-From-East.JPG "El Castillo being climbed by tourists") | 600 AD
[Cuzco Region](/wiki/Cuzco_Region "Cuzco Region"), Peru | [Machu Picchu in Peru](/wiki/File:Machu_Picchu,_Peru.jpg "Machu Picchu in Peru") | 1450 AD

[Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil | [Christ the Redeemer in Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/File:Unique_Moment_with_the_Moon_and_Christ_the_Redeemer_3.jpg "Christ the Redeemer in Rio de Janeiro") | 1931 AD

was a campaign started in 2001 to choose [Wonders of the World](/wiki/Wonders_of_the_World "Wonders of the World") from a selection of 200 existing monuments.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The popularity poll via free web-based voting and telephone voting was led by Canadian-Swiss Bernard Weber[[2]](#cite_note-2) and organized by the New 7 Wonders Foundation (N7W) based in Zurich, Switzerland, with winners announced on 7 July 2007 at [Estádio da Luz](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_Luz "Estádio da Luz") in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The poll was considered unscientific partly because it was possible for people to cast multiple votes.[[7]](#cite_note-LAT-7) According to [John Zogby](/wiki/John_Zogby "John Zogby"), founder and current President/CEO of the US polling organization [Zogby International](/wiki/Zogby_International "Zogby International"), New 7 Wonders Foundation drove "the largest poll on record".[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [New7Wonders of the World](/w/index.php?title=New7Wonders_of_the_World&redirect=no "New7Wonders of the World"))

This article is about the New 7 Wonders Foundation list. For other uses, see [Wonders of the World](/wiki/Wonders_of_the_World "Wonders of the World").

Online popularity poll in 2007 to pick new Wonders of the World

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/New7Wonders.jpg/500px-New7Wonders.jpg)](/wiki/File:New7Wonders.jpg)

From left to right, top to bottom: [Chichén Itzá](/wiki/Chich%C3%A9n_Itz%C3%A1 "Chichén Itzá"), *[Christ the Redeemer](/wiki/Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue) "Christ the Redeemer (statue)")*, the [Great Wall of China](/wiki/Great_Wall_of_China "Great Wall of China"), [Machu Picchu](/wiki/Machu_Picchu "Machu Picchu"), [Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra"), the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal"), and the [Colosseum](/wiki/Colosseum "Colosseum")

The **New 7 Wonders of the World** was a campaign started in 2001 to choose [Wonders of the World](/wiki/Wonders_of_the_World "Wonders of the World") from a selection of 200 existing monuments.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The popularity poll via free web-based voting and telephone voting was led by Canadian-Swiss Bernard Weber[[2]](#cite_note-2) and organized by the New 7 Wonders Foundation (N7W) based in Zurich, Switzerland, with winners announced on 7 July 2007 at [Estádio da Luz](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_da_Luz "Estádio da Luz") in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The poll was considered unscientific partly because it was possible for people to cast multiple votes.[[7]](#cite_note-LAT-7) According to [John Zogby](/wiki/John_Zogby "John Zogby"), founder and current President/CEO of the US polling organization [Zogby International](/wiki/Zogby_International "Zogby International"), New 7 Wonders Foundation drove "the largest poll on record".[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)

The program drew a wide range of official reactions. Some countries touted their finalist and tried to get more votes cast for it, while others downplayed or criticized the contest.[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)[[7]](#cite_note-LAT-7) After supporting the New 7 Wonders Foundation at the beginning of the campaign by providing advice on nominee selection, the United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organization ([UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")), bound by its bylaws to record and give equal status to all [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), distanced itself from the undertaking in 2001 and again in 2007.[[8]](#cite_note-N7W&UNESCO-8)[[9]](#cite_note-UNESCO-9)

The 7 winners were chosen from 21 candidates, which had been whittled down from 77 choices by a panel in 2006.

The New 7 Wonders Foundation, established in 2001, relied on private donations and the sale of broadcast rights and received no public funding.[[10]](#cite_note-new7wonders.com-10) After the final announcement, New 7 Wonders said it did not earn anything from the exercise and barely recovered its investment.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Although N7W describes itself as a not-for-profit organization, the company behind it, the New Open World Corporation (NOWC), is a commercial business. All licensing and sponsorship money is paid to NOWC.

The foundation ran two subsequent programs: [New 7 Wonders of Nature](/wiki/New_7_Wonders_of_Nature "New 7 Wonders of Nature"), the subject of voting until 2011, and [New 7 Wonders Cities](/wiki/New_7_Wonders_Cities "New 7 Wonders Cities"), which ended in 2014.

## Winners

## Reactions given

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Reactions given")]

### United Nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: United Nations")]

In 2007, the New 7 Wonders Foundation contracted a partnership with the United Nations in recognition of the efforts to promote the UN's [Millennium Development Goals](/wiki/Millennium_Development_Goals "Millennium Development Goals").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

However, the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), in a press release on June 20, 2007, reaffirmed that it has no link with the initiative. The press release concluded:[[9]](#cite_note-UNESCO-9)

> There is no comparison between Mr Weber's mediatised campaign and the scientific and educational work resulting from the inscription of sites on UNESCO's [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). The list of the "7 New Wonders of the World" will be the result of a private undertaking, reflecting only the opinions of those with access to the internet and not the entire world. This initiative cannot, in any significant and sustainable manner, contribute to the preservation of sites elected by this public.

— UNESCO

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Brazil")]

In Brazil there was a campaign *Vote no Cristo* (Vote for the Christ) which had the support of private companies, namely telecommunications operators that stopped charging voters to make telephone calls and SMS messages to vote.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Additionally, leading corporate sponsors including [Banco Bradesco](/wiki/Banco_Bradesco "Banco Bradesco") and [Rede Globo](/wiki/Rede_Globo "Rede Globo") spent millions of [reals](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real") in the effort to have the statue voted into the top seven.[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5) *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* reports the campaign was so pervasive that:[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)

> One morning in June, Rio de Janeiro residents awoke to a beeping text message on their cell phones: "Press 4916 and vote for Christ. It's free!" The same pitch had been popping up all over the city since late January—flashing across an electronic screen every time city-dwellers swiped their transit cards on city buses and echoing on TV infomercials that featured a reality-show celebrity posing next to the city's trademark Christ the Redeemer statue.

— Elizabeth Dwoskin, *Newsweek*

According to an article in *Newsweek*, around 10 million Brazilians had voted in the contest by early July.[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5) This number is estimated as the New 7 Wonders Foundation never released such details about the campaign. An airplane message, with a huge inscription "4916 VOTE FOR CHRIST" flew in Rio de Janeiro for a month.

### Peru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Peru")]

An intensive campaign led by the Peruvian [Ministry of Commerce and Tourism](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Trade_and_Tourism_(Peru) "Ministry of Foreign Trade and Tourism (Peru)") in Peru had a great impact in the media and consequently, Peruvian people voted massively for its national wonder. The announcement of the new World Wonders generated great expectations and the election of [Machu Picchu](/wiki/Machu_Picchu "Machu Picchu") was celebrated nationwide.

### Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chile")]

The Chilean representative for [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island")'s [Moais](/wiki/Moai "Moai"), Alberto Hortus, said Weber gave him a letter saying that the Moais had finished eighth and were morally one of the New 7 Wonders. Hortus said he was the only participant to receive such an apology.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: India")]

A campaign to publicize the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal") in India gathered speed and it reached a climax in July 2007 with news channels, radio stations, and many celebrities asking people to vote for the Taj Mahal.

### Jordan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Jordan")]

[Queen Rania of Jordan](/wiki/Queen_Rania_of_Jordan "Queen Rania of Jordan") joined the campaign to back [Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra"), Jordan's national treasure.[[5]](#cite_note-nwVFC-5)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_7_Wonders_of_the_World&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mexico")]

There was a campaign on the news programs to encourage people to vote for [Chichen Itzá](/wiki/Chichen_Itz%C3%A1 "Chichen Itzá").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
